speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do have any other languages? September 22, 1995; Albert Rosa. Date, September 22, 1995; survivor, Albert Rosa; interviewer, Tama Bader Freund; city/state, Encino, California; country, United States of America; language, English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. My name as Tama Bader Freund-- T-A-M-A, B-A-D-E-R, F-R-E-U-N-D. Today's date is Friday, September 22, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Albert Rosa, in Encino, California, in the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Albert Rosa-- A-L-B-E-R-T, R-O-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Rosa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January the 25th, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 70 years now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Greece, Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] OK. Can you tell me what your life was like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war-- I-- I come from a religious family. My father was very orthodox. I wasn't. I-- my father had a hardware store-- which we was middle class people-- probably a little bit upper middle class. And-- and I was a young teenager going to school. I was very much involved in sports. When I was-- my brother was one of the big shots in the Hakoah. It's an Jewish organization for sports. I-- he-- he took me when I was 10 years old. I'm not allowed to be there that young, but he sneaked me in. I used to box there, play soccer, ping pong, and swimming. And we used to compete with different other people, especially with the non-Jews. Because the reason we have to learn how to box-- in case we have to defend ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a very nice life. My-- by Friday, by the night-- Shabbes-- we used to come the whole family together and to make the Kaddish. You know, like I-- like I said, they were Orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have very good life till the war started in 1939, in Greece, against the Italians. So the Italians bombed all over Greece. The Italians was losing the war. Then they-- then they called help-- for the Germans to come over there to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans come over, a small country like Greece, they couldn't survive against Germany. I think there was about nine months in the war against the Germans. And finally, they lost. The Germans occupy that-- Greece. They occupy Salonika.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then when the problem starts. One day-- one day my father went to open the business. He find on the door a piece of paper. What it says in German, it says, while anybody will remove this paper, they will be executed on the spot. Otherwise, they went there, they become owners of my father's property. They took everything-- what's inside the store, and they ship it to Germany-- every up to the last nail. So we had no business, and we have to struggle, you know, to survive. And-- and from there after-- after a while, they start taking the young people to work for the Germans, to make roads. And were-- we forced-- forced slave labor to make roads and different things for the German. We're working about a year for them, probably a little longer. I don't remember exactly. I was one of them. People start dying from typhus and all kinds of things. It was mistreating, beating. And finally, the Jewish community-- it was quite a few of wealthy people-- they offer a large amount of money to the Germans to release us, you know, from this slavery. So-- then they release. And a few months later, they lock us in ghettos, because we were living in sections of Jewish neighborhoods together. And I got locked in my-- in my-- my neighborhood, it was about 10,000 Jews-- and where I was born and grew up there. And they lock us up in terror. After a-- after a few months later, they start taking and shipping-- they taking people, start sending to Auschwitz. We don't know at the time where we was going to go. They told us a lie. There was a repossessing a different place till the war ends. And they took everything we had. They gave us a phony paper that said what you got-- so-and-so. They multiply in-- in marks. You're going to get this money back in where you're going. So-- so the-- I was the second transport from Salonika.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was sometimes in-- around Passover. It was March or April, 1943. And they-- they make us-- they come in the morning in the house. They start kicking with the rifle and everything. And it all doesn't matter how old you was. If I was 1 year old, if I was 90 years old, they start beating ups everybody. They put us-- they put us in trucks, and they took us to the station. And the station over there, there was the cattle trains, like they put the animals in, with a little teeny window. They shipped in about around 80 to 90 people in each train, one on top on the other one. And they had a one bucket of water. They were around the corner. We don't even know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were 13 days on the train-- suffocated. We couldn't even breathe. Quite a few older people die.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know where to go the bathroom, no nothing. There was a little window. Everybody has to fight to get to the window over there to breathe, to get a little air.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so the people who knew there was water there, they drink it. The other people, some people they just-- 13 days, no water, no nothing, you know? Some-- quite a few die. So I was young and strong. I made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we come to-- when we come to Auschwitz-- Salonika is a very mild weather. It's not a very-- it doesn't-- it's not-- it doesn't get very cold. When we come to Auschwitz, we come go from the-- from the wagon, the train, the cattle place, you know-- kicking us to come out. And snow is up to the knee. We not used to that thing. People start freezing to death. From theres, they took us to the camp, and they start to-- to separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Who were you with in the cattle--

speaker: interviewee
text: I were-- we were separate. I was, at the time, I was with my two brothers and-- yeah, my-- my two brothers. My-- my sister, she had two children, and she was pregnant-- my oldest sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luna Rosa-- Lena. And-- and-- they-- she-- because she was pregnant, my mother took one of the babies to hold, and the husband for her to the other one. Because she was unable to carry the baby. So-- so by accident, they put them in her also. They went with the people to work in the concentration camp. So the-- my mother and father took her away to the orphan. I didn't know at the time. I find out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So also, my brother also had one child, my oldest brother, David. And his wife was also pregnant, and also she went also to the crematorium. But David, when-- when-- I saw him in the camp for a short while, Then we got separated. And I-- I hanged together with Daniel, which is the other brother. So let me see. Also, my father and mother, because they were already-- my father was already about-- about in their early 60s, they put-- they-- they went right away to a crematorium. Anybody over 35, they will never make it. They put 'em-- they-- they think they was too old to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was, at time, about maybe about 14 or 15, but I-- I was a very-- I-- well, the same size I am now. I was a big, husky guy at the time, because I was an athlete. So I passed me for a grown up person. And that's why they don't put me to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the camp, I-- I-- I-- I got together with my other brother, Daniel. And we got to get together as much as we can. And they put us-- labor. We saw something burning, smelling terrible, but we don't know what it was. We don't know, you know, what's-- it was the crematoriums burning in the gas chamber. And we work in different [? hall ?] making canals and all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the food was unbelievable. It was terrible. There were-- [LAUGHS] we have to look to find a little potato inside in the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, a friend of mines told me, you know, I was in certain commando, and I saw your sister, Luna. I saw her. But she looked pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes, she was pregnant. He-- I said, I want to see her, I say. He says to me, well, I don't know how you're gonna see her. I said, listen. I said, I'm gonna give you my two-day portions-- rations, what I eat. Just give me your jacket. Because in the jacket, we used to have the same number we got on the arm, you know. And they're supposed to meet, the two numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That way I can sneak in, you know? I go. You go your place. You-- and you go in my commando, we say. So we took a chance. If they catch us, who knows what's going to happen? Or we'll get killed or something. And so he-- he gave me his jacket, and I took it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I gave my jacket to him. He gave me a jacket for-- for one day. So-- and I gave him my two day's food what I used to get. I have to-- I have to go not to eat for two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, and I-- and I-- I look around. And finally, I saw my sister. And I started hollering to her. And there was some-- by that fence. So I went close to it. And a German SS woman saw it-- threw the dog on me-- because I tried to talk to her. And he got me a good bite on the leg. As this is the last time I saw my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there, I went back to camp. And I gave the jacket to the guy, back. And-- and-- and there was the end of it-- that far. So then I was working with my brother different places-- tried to stick together. Because we didn't know the rest of the family what happened. Were you in the same barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same barracks, yeah. We tried to stick-- every time they tried to separate us, we tried to-- to stick together, you know? They said 10 this side, 20 this side. We-- we used to go together. Because out there, everybody else-- gone. So-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we were working in a-- in a-- in a place over there, very hard labor, and digging holes, and everything. And then I saw they were-- unloaded a-- a truck of potatoes-- a-- a wagon with horses. They used to bring the potatoes to the kitchen. I was so hungry, you know? I couldn't standed the hunger anymore. I-- my feet were swelling and everything. It was about five, six months after that-- that we were in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] So-- so I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't resist the hunger. I was-- I was seeing things, you know, like, mirage in front of me. I mean, hunger and hungry is different, where you eat one day, two days is hunger. But when you don't eat for months in and months out, this is what hunger is. You-- everything you see, seems like it's food. Even-- even a-- a round stone looks like food, like it is a potato.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took a rush over there, and I run into the-- to the thing over there. I look around, and nobody is watching me. I run, and I grab a couple of potatoes from over there, and I tried to run back to the-- to the-- where the people were working, digging.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was an Ukrainian-- Ukrainian-- the one he was escaped people from-- from the Russia army. And he come voluntarily to the German army-- an Ukrainian soldier. But he become an-- an-- an SS soldier. He saw me doing that. He took-- he put the rifle to shoot me. The gun jam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he turned the gun around, and he starts swinging the gun at me like-- like I was hitting a-- a pillow-- those heavy, old fashioned guns. He hit me one in they knee, and the knee was totally dislocated. The knee got this side. So then two or three times I-- I-- the-- I dive a few times not to hit. Then he was-- he hit me right with it, right on the mouth, here. And my tooth is in the front, it was hanging down like a-- from a-- hanging down, you know. I-- I thought he knocked me all my teeth out.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was watching. He couldn't take it no more. All of a-- [SOB] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: --all of a sudden, then he went crazy. He attacked the German. And he punched him. And he put him down on the ground. He start punching him all over the face. He knocked him out. Before you know it, another 20 soldiers jump on top of him-- guards, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They beat him up almost three-quarters dead. Then from there, they took him to the camp, and they hang him. And I was-- and I saw with my own eyes. There was then that my-- the last of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, from there, I was in-- I was in Birkenau about eight or nine months. In Poland, there was there-- I-- I was in Aufstand, where the-- where the Jews fought against the Germans in the ghetto. They were looking for some people where they don't speak Polish or German. That way they-- they'd be more safe, because the camp was not very much secure, like in Birkenau. It was electric wires power around, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was [NON-ENGLISH], people-- the people that don't understand the language, and so forth. So they pick up a thousand Greek Jews from Birkenau to take 'em to Warsaw to-- to do some kind of labor. I don't know what kind of labor it was going to be at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start count. They start taking five by five-- check 'em over to see who-- which one was strong and which wasn't-- who was healthy. So some guys, they knew at Birkenau there was no way you can survive. To get anywhere else outside Birkenau, which is Auschwitz, Birkenau was about four miles different. And some people tried to go from the other line. So in that time, they saw them, other people jumping in, you know, to the-- the-- the right side, where it's supposed to go to Warsaw. We don't know where it was going to take it. I find out later it was Warsaw. So that-- that-- and then the Germans start beating them up, you know, and threw the big German Shepherds on top of it. I find a chance, a time, and I sneak on the side, you know. And I went-- and I went with the-- with the healthy, strong people. But I was not healthy and strong.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- now I don't know if I should say something about my cousin. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Before I went to Warsaw, I was almost like a dead person. I was swelling everywhere with no food or anything. And I had one cousin left, which his name was the same name as mines-- Albert Rose. He-- he went to-- to Vienna, and he study. He study to become some kind of-- I don't know what it was. I was a young kid at the time. He was about nine-- about nine or eight years older than me, I think. And-- and-- and he spoke German pretty good, because he went-- he spent some years in-- in Austria, and he learned the German language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- he-- because he spoke the-- the language good, German, he had it very good. He was an interpreter, which they call them [GERMAN] in German. So he-- to explain to the Greeks what the Germans say. So then he got a job in the kitchen, the head chef in the kitchen. People was telling me, Albert, why don't you go in your cousin and ask him to help you, you know? You're dying.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a very proud person. I said, no. I don't want nobody's help. If I make it on my own, all right. If I don't, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, people are telling me, you're crazy, stupid. What's the matter with you? The guy-- the guy, you know, is doing so good. After all, he's flesh and blood. His brother and my brother was-- I mean, his father and my father was brothers. So I went to him to ask him. And the guy-- I don't know what happened to him. He was a nice person before the war when he used to come by Saturday, by night, in our house and everything, you know? He used to. And I asked him. I say, Albert. I say, would you please help me? Look on this condition I am. I say, I cannot take it no more, the punishment, the beating. I haven't been getting the food, the hard labor. I say, I'm not used to those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, get the hell out of here. He says, don't bother me. He says to me, don't you ever call me I'm your cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, you remember how much my father helped you when you was going to college? He turn around. I say, you're no good. I say, one day I'm gonna get even with you. He turned around, and he beat the hell out of me-- knocked me down. I-- I could not standed on my feet. He was older than me. I was a youngster. I was 15, 16 years old, you know, and weak. Maybe I weighed by that time, about 75, 80 pounds, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He kicked me. I couldn't even get up from the floor after he finished beating me. Then I tell him, Albert, I said, God willing, if I get out of here, one day I gonna get even with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and then-- and then that-- I forgot something to say. When-- when we went in the camp over there, a lot of senior people, you know, in their 30s, 40s, you know, they find out their wive and the kids-- the kids they-- they got-- they got killed and everything else. They say, it's no-- what's the use living? I go and throw myself in the wires-- on the electric wires, and burn myself-- electrocute. There's no use living with that kind of life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I went, and I tell them, listen. I say, you have to try to survive just to pay back to the Germans. Don't give up. Try to fight and-- to stay alive. One day, God willing, we get out of here, and we're going to get even with those Germans. There's something we're going to do to repay them back. Don't give up. There are some-- some did listen to me, some they didn't. A matter of fact, I meet two or three guys after the war that said thanks for you. So I-- I am alive today, because I was ready to give up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So coming back to the place in Warsaw, the-- from there, we walked to-- to Auschwitz. It was a very short time-- place from Birkenau. We spent a couple nights in Auschwitz. Which in Auschwitz, it was nice barracks, not like-- like in Birkenau. Birkenau was-- was a very bad place. So we had-- we stayed there two weeks. They put us on the train. And they took us to Warsaw. We went in Warsaw over there. They count, and we see if we got 1,000 people there. They counted over, they find 1,001. They count again, 1,001.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- back and forth, they keep us about two, three hours counting back and forth. The extra person was me-- the 1,001. It's supposed to be [LAUGHS] 1,000. So I took an extra trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we come to Warsaw. It was very cold. It gets very cold there. And we find out, where is the camp? This is the camp-- an open field-- no barracks, no water, no nothing. So the following morning, they took us in a place over there where the ghetto was. We saw thousands of corpses on the floor, on the-- rotting on the floor with worms, flies, and everything, you know? And they make us, the Germans, to pick them up with bare hands, you know, rotten and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to take windows and doors from the-- from the building the bomb-- the Germans during the Aufstand. We call them the Aufstand. But the uprise, it was. And we used to burn them. And we were doing it for a few-- three, four months. We were burning the-- the corpses from the people. There was all kind of ages, from one year, from one month, till 80, 90 years old people, all ages. It was there. We burned them up. So-- and the smell was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then one part of our people was doing that, some other-- the other part was building the barracks for where to stay. So Warsaw was not so bad-- not as bad as Birkenau. After that-- and-- and my job in Warsaw was, they call him the spring commando. The spring commando is to bomb down the-- when the bomb-- the Germans, they fought with the Jews in Warsaw, it was part of the houses was standing. It's one in one wall, some in two walls, you know? And some was falling down by themselves, because there was very much from the bombs. So they put us over there to-- to make a hole in the wall and put the sticks of dynamites inside. The Germans themselves were afraid, because the wall may fall down. So they send ourselves. Somebody-- the wall fell down, it's no big deal. Another Jew die-- no big deal. So some of them-- people I knew there-- you know, you go. You put the stick in, and you run. Because the guy puts the thing on, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some-- sometimes, I was very close. Sometimes the-- the building almost fall on top of me. And sometimes it missed me. So we did that till we clean up the whole-- everything that-- the whole-- the whole Warsaw ghetto. We make it flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what had happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fight between the Germans and the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I didn't. I find out later, much later. So-- so-- and after they build the thing, the Germans, they want the bricks to take it to Germany in their-- in their thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did is we have to clean up the bricks and pile 'em up to 250 each. Even then the-- all the clean bricks with that-- a hammer. And the-- the Polish Christians, they used to come with their horses and their wagon. And they used to-- we have to load the bricks and take it to the train to-- to send it to Germany. So the good part of it was there, we was around the rubbles-- the rubble from the-- from the fall down. There was a-- we find there, like, silver spoons, silver-- silver knives, sometimes gold. And the-- and-- and the Polacks that-- the Christian Polacks that-- we start talking to them. We ask 'em, you wanna-- you wanna a spoon, a silver spoon? He who goes into the city, he could go and sell it for a much higher price-- but to Warsaw, if he it goes out from the gate, from the thing. He said, yeah. So bring me a bread. So they-- they-- they help us to combine our feedback, you know, it was-- they're changing. We were finding whatever, digging there. We were-- and I-- we would blow the thing, find the spoon-- silver, whatever we can. They give-- they give us a salami, bread. We were doing business with them. So little by little, I started getting back to my-- my strength. The only thing that was wrong with me, I was to have no food to eat. Besides that, I was very healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but in the beginning, I forgot to tell you. When we got there, we got-- from the filth and everything, we had no water, no everything-- the lice was coming on top of us like a-- like an army-- going all over, even inside the food. You know, the bread we used to have, the lice was going inside. we used to eat it with the lice and everything. You know, it's because it was for four or five months we didn't have no water, no place to sleep. We have to build the barracks. And people-- and then from the sickness over there, we got lice typhus. They call them that. People was dying like flies. From the Greeks-- not too many left-- from the picking up the dead people from the floor with their empty hands, wounds and everything, you know, infected. And quite a few die-- 3/4 of 'em die. Then after a while we got some Hungarian transport coming from Hungary, from Czech. You know, then the camps are getting larger, more people coming in-- not Greeks, but from different countries. It was sometimes in the beginning of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after beginning of 1944, I thinks, the-- the Russian was coming. So what the-- when the Russian come in, then they want to take it out from Warsaw and take us somewhere in Germany. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so one day, we are-- they count around about 23,000 people were there in-- in Warsaw at the time, in the ghetto We built two camps after the Warsaw-- come in Czech Jews, and Hungarian Jews, Jews from France, from Italy, from different parts of the country. In the beginning, only was the Greeks there. But later on, they come from different nationalities.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went there. They make us to get up and to go to Warsaw-- no train, no nothing, because everything, the trains they had, they were using for themselves to take out their items, to get out from there. Because the Russian were not too far from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to walk to Warsaw. I think it's about 950 kilometers. They gave us one loaf of bread and about a liter of [? hank. ?] And that's gonna to be the food for the-- we don't know what-- if-- if this was for one day, or if it's gonna be for, [LAUGHS] you know, for the whole transport. We didn't even know how many days it was gonna be on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was so hungry. I ate everything in one day. And that's it. Then I want to drink. There was no water to drink. They're going crazy-- the [? salt ?] [? hank. ?] So we start walking. We're walking, and put in the line, and keep us walking. About a couple of-- about 100 miles from Warsaw was a big river. I don't know the name of the river. I think it's the [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm not sure-- a very big river-- I would say maybe about 300, 400 feet wide.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- and they make us to cross. They don't want us to go on top on in the-- in the brick over there, because they had the machine guns on top-- the-- the Nazis. So some of us didn't know how to swim. Not everybody knew how to swim. And in the river, it was kind of beginning of winter, even block of ice still was flowing on top in the river there. You can see the-- so-- so then the SS tell 'em go in. Anybody who won't go in, they start shooting. So I went. I went. I was a good swimmer. I learned in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- and I start swimming across the river. In the same time, about 20 people, they grab me from the neck, from the arms, from the legs. You know, want to hold something. They don't know how to swim. So I start punching left and right to liberate myself. So finally, about 3/4 [LAUGHS] dead, I arrived to the other side of the river, where the other Nazis was waiting over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you see thousands of people flying in the-- on top in the river-- the corpses-- people who got drowned, who got shot, the one that cannot-- they were afraid to go in the water. So I don't know what happened to those people-- to the-- either they picked them out or they left them over. I don't know. So then they put us together and tell us, keep marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Germans get across?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the bridge. They had the machine guns on top. And-- and we-- we had to go across through the river. The bridge was not a very big thing, you know? And they didn't want also the civilian to see very much. You know, they tried to hide as much as they can. And so from there, we-- we start walking again, and walking. On the river, what I did is, I soaked my clothes good before I left there. That way, I figure-- but I think I can-- when I get thirsty, I can suck the-- the jacket or anything, and have something to drink-- something-- that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have to continue that on the next--
